text: A twelve millimeter assistant port is placed laterally in the right lower quadrant of the patient's abdomen.
After gaining abdominal access, the surgeon identifies the ovary, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and ensures the proper placement of a stent within the structure.
The ovary is suspended away from the operative field, revealing the broad ligament as the intended entry point into the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon parietalizes the peritoneum of the broad ligament, creating a visible preperitoneal space.
The surgeon carefully confirms that the opposite side of the broad ligament remains intact while entering the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon employs cautious dissection techniques to avoid injury to the retroperitoneal vessels, including the internal iliac vein, while working within the exposed retroperitoneal space.
The sacrum is visible in the surgical field, identified as a point of fixation for the mesh during the procedure.
The dissection progresses, revealing the contour of the sciatic hernia within the exposed retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon reduces the content of the sciatic hernia sac, visibly decreasing its size within the surgical field.
The hernia is shown with its contents fully reduced, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac vein and artery before placing and sewing the mesh, ensuring these vessels are clearly visible and avoiding injury during the procedure.
The sacrum is fully dissected, revealing the area that will serve as the posteromedial point for mesh fixation.
The fibers of the piriformis muscle are visible, marking the posterolateral point for mesh fixation in the surgical field.

The surgeon deploys a hybrid mesh composed of one layer of permanent mesh and an absorbable layer over the dissected area, preparing for fixation at the previously identified posterolateral and posteromedial points.
A large mesh is deployed over the dissected area, providing coverage that will be trimmed to fit the specific dimensions of the surgical field.
The absorbable layer of the hybrid mesh is oriented medially, positioned to protect delicate retroperitoneal structures, including the sciatic nerve.
The surgeon interposes absorbable mesh between the ureter and permanent mesh, visually reducing the risk of ureteral erosion, adhesion, and obstruction.
The surgeon sutures the hybrid mesh to the sacrum using IIO permanent sutures, securing it in place.
The surgeon fixes the hybrid mesh, composed of permanent and absorbable layers, to the piriformis muscle, ensuring secure attachment to the anatomical structure.
The surgeon completes the fixation of the posterior edge of the mesh and trims it to fit the dimensions of the dissection pocket, ensuring optimal coverage.
The surgeon fixes the mesh to the external obturator muscle, which serves as the anterolateral point of fixation.
The surgeon secures the final anteromedial point of the mesh to the levator ani muscles, completing the fixation process.
The surgeon surveys the mesh to confirm it is properly positioned and lays flat within the surgical space after completing fixation.
The surgeon encloses the peritoneal defect using a running barbed absorbable suture, ensuring the defect is securely closed.
